region_of_origin: Middle East / Central Asia (widely cultivated globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of whole grain wheat is approximately 80 g (about half a cup of raw kernels), which expands to roughly 200 g when cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Wheat, whole grain — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Wheat, whole grain (uncooked). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from HPB with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #169721 (Wheat, hard red winter) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Whole grain wheat kernels (wheat berries) GI reported as approximately 41–48 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). A midpoint value of 45 is used. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 45 × 46.6 / 100 ≈ 21.0 g (based on 80 g dry serving providing ~58.3 × 0.8 = 46.6 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), high potassium (315 mg/100 g), and high dietary fibre (11.4 g/100 g) supporting DASH diet targets
hypertension.guidance: Whole grain wheat is well-suited for hypertension management; its negligible sodium content, favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (~157:1), and high fibre align with DASH dietary guidelines for blood pressure reduction — consume as a regular wholegrain staple in place of refined grains.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (58.3 g/100 g) moderated by substantial dietary fibre (11.4 g/100 g), protein (11.2 g/100 g), and low GI (~45) reducing postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole grain wheat has a low GI (~45) and high fibre content that significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response compared to refined wheat; limit dry serving to 60–80 g per meal and avoid combining with added sugars or refined flour to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (11.4 g/100 g), low saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and beneficial micronutrients (magnesium, potassium, B vitamins) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of whole grain wheat is associated with reduced LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk; its high soluble and insoluble fibre, low saturated fat, and zero cholesterol make it a cardioprotective staple grain suitable for daily inclusion in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome diversity and immune regulation; contains gluten which may trigger sensitivity in a subset of RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Whole grain wheat may support immune regulation via gut microbiome modulation due to its high fibre content; however, patients with confirmed gluten sensitivity or coeliac disease must avoid it, and RA patients should monitor individual symptom response to gluten-containing grains.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Whole grain wheat (Triticum aestivum) consists of intact wheat kernels — also called wheat berries — with all three anatomical components preserved: the outer bran layer, the nutrient-dense germ, and the starchy endosperm. In its raw, uncooked state it is a hard, dry grain with a golden-brown colour. When cooked by boiling or simmering (typically 45–60 minutes), the kernels soften to a firm, chewy texture with a nutty, earthy flavour and a dense, substantial consistency. Whole grain wheat is a globally cultivated staple grain originating in the Fertile Crescent of the Middle East and Central Asia, now consumed across South Asia, the Middle East, Europe, and increasingly in Southeast Asia as a health-conscious alternative to refined grains. Nutritionally, per 100 g uncooked it provides 326 kcal, 11.2 g protein, 2.4 g fat (of which only 0.4 g is saturated), 58.3 g carbohydrate, 11.4 g dietary fibre, and just 2 mg sodium. Its high fibre content supports digestive health, glycaemic control, and cardiovascular protection. It is a meaningful source of iron (3.43 mg), potassium (315 mg), magnesium (138 mg), phosphorus (357 mg), zinc (2.65 mg), B vitamins (thiamine, niacin, B6, folate), and vitamin E. Its glycemic index is approximately 45 (low GI), making it significantly more suitable for blood glucose management than refined wheat flour or white bread. Whole grain wheat is naturally free of cholesterol and very low in sodium, aligning with DASH and heart-healthy dietary guidelines. It contains gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. It is plant-based, vegan, halal, and kosher. Common culinary uses include cooking as a whole grain side dish, adding to grain salads, soups, and stews, or sprouting for use in salads and health foods.

ingredient_synonyms.whole wheat kernels: same as wheat berries; bran, germ and endosperm intact
ingredient_synonyms.hard wheat: higher protein variety of whole grain wheat
ingredient_synonyms.soft wheat: lower protein variety of whole grain wheat
ingredient_synonyms.全麦: Chinese term for whole grain wheat
ingredient_synonyms.gandum penuh: Malay term for whole grain wheat
ingredient_synonyms.साबुत गेहूँ: Hindi term for whole grain wheat
ingredient_synonyms.முழு கோதுமை: Tamil term for whole grain wheat

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #169721 (Wheat, hard red winter) confirming plausibility. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for uncooked whole grain wheat.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) are not reported in the HPB source; values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #169721 (Wheat, hard red winter, raw) and documented as adapted. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; whole grain wheat contains negligible to no industrially produced trans fats — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — naturally zero, as wheat is a plant food containing no cholesterol. 4. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100 g and vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100 g — naturally zero in plant-based grains per USDA FoodData Central. 5. Vitamin C and vitamin A are set to -1.0: whole grain wheat contains negligible vitamin C and vitamin A (RAE) not consistently reported across approved sources; USDA SR Legacy reports 0 mcg RAE and 0 mg vitamin C for raw wheat grain, but HPB does not report these fields — set to -1 to avoid false precision. 6. B vitamins (thiamine 0.38 mg, riboflavin 0.12 mg, niacin 5.46 mg, B6 0.34 mg), folate (44 mcg DFE), vitamin E (1.01 mg), phosphorus (357 mg), magnesium (138 mg), zinc (2.65 mg), selenium (70.7 mcg), and water (10.6 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #169721 as HPB does not report these micronutrients; documented as adapted. 7. GI value of 45 is a midpoint estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range of 41–48 for whole wheat kernels/wheat berries (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 8. This record covers uncooked whole grain wheat only; cooked wheat berries will have different per-100 g values due to water absorption. 9. Whole grain wheat contains gluten and is contraindicated for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
